---
title: "How Voxtral Realtime Works"
---

## From waveform to text

Voxtral Mini 4B Realtime is not “Whisper followed by an unrelated LLM.” It is a
streaming audio-conditioned autoregressive decoder. Audio is transformed into a
sequence of vectors in the text decoder's embedding dimension. At each decoder
position, an audio vector is added to a token embedding.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    Audio["Audio front end<br/>16 kHz PCM → 128-bin log-Mel"]
    Encode["Acoustic representation<br/>causal convolutions → 32-layer encoder<br/>→ 4x grouping and modality adapter"]
    Decode["Conditioned autoregressive decoding<br/>audio + previous-token embeddings<br/>→ 26-layer decoder → next token"]
    Audio --> Encode --> Decode
    Decode -->|next token becomes previous token| Decode
```

## Audio representation

The model expects:

- sample rate: 16 kHz;
- 128 Mel bins;
- FFT window: 400 samples, or 25 ms;
- hop: 160 samples, or 10 ms;
- Mel rate: 100 frames/s.

The convolution stem contains two causal width-3 convolutions. The second has
stride 2. The audio transformer therefore receives approximately 50 positions/s.
The adapter groups four encoder positions, producing:

\[
\frac{100}{2 \cdot 4} = 12.5
\]

audio-conditioned decoder positions per second. One final position therefore
represents 80 ms of source audio. This is why delay and context settings are
converted using 12.5 tokens/s.

## Audio encoder

The encoder has:

| Property | Value |
| --- | ---: |
| layers | 32 |
| model dimension | 1280 |
| query heads | 32 |
| KV heads | 32 |
| head dimension | 64 |
| FFN hidden dimension | 5120 |
| local attention window | 750 positions |

It is causal and uses a rolling KV cache. Each incremental call processes only
new post-convolution positions while retaining keys and values needed by the
local window.

The 750-position encoder window is not the same setting as the application's
decoder restart threshold. At roughly 50 encoder positions/s it covers about 15
seconds of acoustic encoder context.

## Adapter

The adapter consumes groups of four 1280-dimensional encoder vectors. Concatenation
produces \(4 \times 1280 = 5120\) values. Two learned linear projections map:

\[
5120 \rightarrow 3072 \rightarrow 3072
\]

The output dimension matches the text decoder. The stream keeps zero to three
encoder positions as a residual until a complete group of four is available.

## Text decoder

The decoder has:

| Property | Value |
| --- | ---: |
| layers | 26 |
| model dimension | 3072 |
| query heads | 32 |
| KV heads | 8 |
| head dimension | 128 |
| FFN hidden dimension | 9216 |
| sliding attention window | 8192 |
| vocabulary | 131072 |

Eight KV heads with 32 query heads implement grouped-query attention: four query
heads share each key/value head. Token embeddings are tied to the output
projection.

At position \(i\), the decoder input is:

\[
x_i = a_i + E(t_{i-1})
\]

where \(a_i\) is the adapter's audio vector and \(E(t_{i-1})\) is the embedding
of the previous generated token. This direct sum is the core audio/text fusion.

## Initial prompt and delayed streaming

The initial decoder prompt is:

```text
[BOS] [STREAMING_PAD] ... [STREAMING_PAD]
```

with `32 + delay_tokens` pad positions. The C code requires adapter vectors for
all prompt positions before prefill.

`delay_tokens` controls how far token generation trails audio:

\[
d = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{delay milliseconds}}{80} \right\rfloor
\]

The default 320 ms becomes four delay tokens. More delay supplies greater future
acoustic context before a word is committed; less delay can be faster but less
stable.

The delay also conditions every decoder layer. The runtime constructs a
sinusoidal embedding of the numeric delay and passes it through each layer's
small \(3072 \rightarrow 32 \rightarrow 3072\) MLP. The resulting scale is used
by adaptive RMS normalization. Delay is therefore both a prompt offset and a
learned conditioning signal.

## Streaming invariants

Chunked execution must match a logically continuous stream. The runtime retains:

- unfinished Mel analysis state;
- the last two Mel frames for the first convolution;
- the last two convolution outputs for the second convolution;
- one possible stride-alignment residual;
- encoder KV cache;
- zero to three encoder outputs awaiting adapter grouping;
- adapter embeddings not yet consumed by the decoder;
- decoder KV cache and previous text token;
- decoded token queue.

The difficult part is not the transformer equations; it is preserving all
boundary conditions when arbitrary microphone chunk sizes split convolution,
downsampling, and decoder positions.

## Prefill and decode

Once enough adapter vectors exist:

1. build `BOS + pad` token embeddings;
2. add the corresponding adapter vectors;
3. prefill all positions except the last into the KV cache;
4. run the last prompt position to sample the first token;
5. for each later adapter position, add the previous generated token embedding;
6. run one decoder step and enqueue visible text.

The selected runtime uses greedy top-token generation for the visible best path.
It can calculate a small number of probability-near alternatives, but
ClassroomCaptions requests only the best token.

## Continuous recovery

A lecture is much longer than a normal utterance. Continuous mode restarts
decoder state on:

- EOS;
- configured KV threshold;
- 64 consecutive control or invalid tokens;
- 20 seconds of advancing audio without decoder progress.

Repeated empty restarts escalate to a full reset including Mel, convolution,
encoder, adapter, and decoder state. These watchdogs trade context continuity
for the ability to recover during a long live session.

The UI's context control sets a maximum decoder threshold between 250 and 8000
positions. At 12.5 positions/s, the default 2000 is approximately 160 seconds.

## Precision and memory

The official 8.3 GB BF16 safetensors file is memory-mapped. The Metal path
converts and caches weights as FP16 where useful. Decoder KV can use FP16 shared
memory. Small normalization and conditioning arrays remain FP32.

This is not a 4-bit transcription path. The design accepts a larger model
footprint to protect the primary accessibility signal.

## Why silent pre-roll exists

The decoder cannot start until it has enough adapter positions for the initial
prompt. If microphone audio begins immediately, the first several seconds are
used to construct that prompt and appear to be “missing.”

`VoxtralEmbeddedService` feeds 3.2 seconds of silence before reporting
`connected`, drains any generated pieces, and opens the microphone afterward.
The prompt is then ready before real speech arrives.
